steps.title: What Are Conic Sections?
steps.goal: See how a plane slicing through a double cone creates different curves.
steps.introText: Conic sections are shapes created when a flat plane cuts through a double cone. By changing the angle of the plane, we can create circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas. You may have learned about these in your previous math classes, but now we will reinforce your knowledge of these curves and where they come from.
steps.prompt: Toggle the labels to identify the cone, plane, and cross section, then continue when you're ready.

steps.title: Free Exploration
steps.goal: Observe how different slices produce different shapes.
steps.prompt: Move the plane around and watch the cross section update in real time. Whenever you're ready, continue to the next step.

steps.title: Create a Circle
steps.info: A circle forms when the plane is almost horizontal and cuts across one cone — like slicing an ice-cream cone straight across.
steps.goal: Move the plane so that when it intersects the cone, it forms a circle.

feedback.correct: Great! You've made a circle. Nice and simple start.
feedback.incorrect: This slice isn't a circle yet.
feedback.hint: Try making the plane more horizontal (near 0° or 180° or 360°) and keep the plane in the upper or lower cone.

steps.title: Create an Ellipse
steps.goal: Tilt the plane slightly so the cross section becomes an oval.
steps.info: An ellipse appears when the plane is tilted but still shallow enough to cut only one cone. Appearance-wise, think of it as a stretched circle.

feedback.correct: Nice work! You've made an ellipse. Now you can see how the angle of the plane can affect the shape of the cross section.
feedback.incorrect: You're close, but this slice is producing a different conic.
feedback.hint: Start out similar to when you made the circle, but now tilt the plane slightly.

steps.miniReflection: Notice how the circle from before is now stretched into an oval shape.

steps.title: Create a Parabola
steps.goal: Match the plane's angle to the slope of the cone's edge to form a parabola.
steps.info: The parabola is a borderline case: the plane is parallel to the cone's side, so it still only cuts through one cone. For the purposes of this lesson, we will have a slight range where we will consider the plane close enough to being parallel.

feedback.correct: Excellent! A parabola is a bit harder to form, but you just have to remember to tilt the plane to the same angle as the cone's edge.
feedback.hint: Make sure the slope of the plane is similar to the slope of the cone's edge. You can try moving the plane to the upper cone then tilting it to an angle around 53° or 127°.

steps.title: Create a Hyperbola
steps.goal: Make the plane steep enough so it cuts through both cones.
steps.info: A hyperbola is the most complex conic section. It has two separate branches because the plane is angled strongly enough to slice through both halves of the double cone.

feedback.correct: Perfect! Because the plane cuts both cones, the cross section becomes a hyperbola.
feedback.hint: A hyperbola can be a bit tricky. Try adjusting your angle to align your plane with the dotted line.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.goal: Connect what you did with the big idea behind conic sections.
steps.prompt: What determines which conic section appears?

choices.label: The vertical position of the plane

steps.feedback: Exactly. When you were trying to form each cross section, the main thing that you had to do was adjust the angle of the plane. Changing the vertical position would affect the size of the cross section, but did not really affect its shape.
steps.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Think about what you had to change during the previous challenges to get the shape you wanted.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Prove you can create all four conic sections without hints.
steps.completionMessage: You've discovered all four conic sections! In the next lesson, we'll focus on parabolas to learn more about their key features and how they are defined.
